label: SES-029 v0.5 Conversation 2 closeout: 7 decisions, 1 session, 0 planning items, 7 references

session.title: v0.5 Conversation 2 — build planning (release PRD, implementation plan, five slice build prompts)
session.conversation_reference: Claude.ai conversation on 05-16-26 that opened against the seed prompt at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/v0.5-conversation-2-kickoff.md. Translated v0.5 Conversation 1's two deliverables (multi-engagement-architecture.md and methodology-schema-specs/engagement.md) into a build plan. Settled the ten Conversation-2-deferred questions cataloged in the kickoff §5.1 (the two UI-shape questions for switching and management affordances; single-gesture creation+activation behavior; soft-delete-on-active-engagement posture; default value for engagement_export_dir on new engagements; the engagement_last_opened_at PATCH path during the switching dance; engagement panel refresh trigger; activation-sequence retry/timeout values; slice placement of the dogfood migration; and meta DB export location). Produced ui-PRD-v0.5.md, ui-v0.5-implementation-plan.md, and the five slice build prompts at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.5-{A..E}-*.md. Three-way numbering collision with the parallel PI-001 styling workstream resolved at closeout: styling Conversation 1 (SES-027) close-out applied first claiming DEC-087-094; styling Conversation 2 ran in parallel with this conversation and claimed SES-028 plus DEC-095-097 for its v0.6 PRD authoring; this conversation therefore claims SES-029 and DEC-098-104. Mechanical bookkeeping per the v0.4 SES-016 → SES-017 precedent. No transcript preserved per DEC-025.
session.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/v0.5-conversation-2-kickoff.md against the project default ARCHITECTURE operating mode.

Pre-flight. Read crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md (684 lines; particular attention to the API envelope contract {data, meta, errors} added at commit 9b6fcdc and the prefixed-identifier client-side-computation rule); PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/multi-engagement-architecture.md from Conversation 1 (the 12-step activation sequence in §4 and the two-database API server detail in §3.10 plus the five Conversation-2-deferred questions in §6.1); PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-schema-specs/engagement.md (the engagement entity schema and the five Conversation-2-deferred questions in §3.8.1); PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/v0.5-engagement-management-workstream-plan.md (the four-slice strawman in §5 and the alternative slicings); ui-PRD-v0.4.md and ui-v0.4-implementation-plan.md (shape templates for the v0.5 release deliverables); the User Process Guide / Engagement Playbook v0.1 at PRDs/process/v2-user-process-guide.md committed at 7885cfe (data layer described as a single SQLite file v2.db; v0.5 introduces multi-engagement routing, so v0.2 update is needed but deferred to v0.6 per the in-flight workstream).

Surfaced numbering-collision context. The parallel PI-001 styling workstream's Conversation 1 (SES-027) closed earlier the same day at 05-16-26; its close-out payload at close-out-payloads/ses_027.json claims SES-027 plus DEC-087 through DEC-094 (eight decisions covering density, color palette, typography, theme keying, modal elevation, icon library Lucide, selected-state vocabulary, and the styling design pass approval). The styling Conversation 2 ran in parallel and committed ui-PRD-v0.6.md plus ui-v0.6-implementation-plan.md mid-way through this conversation; styling Conversation 2 claims SES-028 plus DEC-095-097 (version-bundling as v0.6 separate from v0.5, six-slice structure A-F, per-slice screenshot + WCAG check acceptance pattern). This conversation's payload therefore claims SES-029 plus DEC-098-104 — collision resolution applied at close after the styling apply landed.

Q1+Q2 (consequential decision, paired). UI affordance placement for switching and for management. Three options framed: A — top-strip switching widget + Engagements sidebar group with one entry + dual paths to management; B — sidebar-only with everything in the Engagements group; C — modal-only switching with sidebar group for management. Option A approved. Rationale: switching is a frequent action that needs always-visible affordance distinct from the sidebar's content-navigation function; the Engagements sidebar group entry is the discoverability path for management; dual paths to the management panel (sidebar entry + 'Manage engagements...' picker footer) reduce friction without creating navigational ambiguity. The picker dropdown lives below the top-strip with engagements ordered by engagement_last_opened_at descending, paused/archived sorted to bottom in muted color, active marked with Lucide check icon; footer item opens management panel. Sidebar group positioned above Governance per the v2-install-level scope distinction.

Q3 (consequential decision). Single-gesture engagement creation+activation. Two options: A — one click runs POST + DB file creation + activation behind the scenes with graceful failure recovery; B — two-gesture path with explicit 'Create' then 'Activate' affordances. Option A approved. The New Engagement dialog runs three sequential operations on Submit (POST /engagements → desktop creates per-engagement DB file with Alembic at head → desktop initiates 12-step activation). Three-label progress indicator advances through the operations. Activation failure after both creates succeed surfaces inline with 'Try switching now' / 'Stay in <previous>' affordances; engagement record persists. Validation errors at each stage roll back appropriately. Rationale: matches user mental model (creating an engagement implies starting work in it); the failure-recovery affordances handle the one realistic partial-success state cleanly; the alternative two-gesture flow would force every creator to choose 'now or later' twice which is friction without payoff.

Q9 (consequential decision, refined from kickoff strawman). Slice breakdown. Workstream-plan strawman had four slices (foundation+migration combined, engagement entity panel, switching, closeout); alternative granular framing had six slices (config foundation, dogfood migration, schema+access+REST, panel UI, switching, closeout). Decided five slices: A foundation+migration combined (because foundation alone is non-functional intermediate state), B engagement schema+access layer+REST API, C engagement management panel UI, D switching mechanism+top-strip+picker+single-gesture creation+activation flow, E closeout. Slice A monitored for size at execution time; A1/A2 split fallback documented. Rationale for splitting B from C: B's REST API surface (eight standard endpoints with validation) plus C's panel UI (master/detail panel plus CRUD dialogs plus forbid-active-delete) are each substantial; combining them produces an oversize prompt. Rationale for D as one slice rather than splitting picker from activation worker: the picker and activation worker are tightly coupled (picker click triggers activation worker; activation overlay binds to worker progress signals; the single-gesture flow uses the activation worker as its third step); splitting would require defining a fake activation API in slice C/D and a backfill in the next slice.

Decide-and-announce decisions. Q4 — Forbid soft-delete on active engagement: delete dialog refuses with inline redirect to switch first; last-engagement edge case uses 'create another first' wording. The drift-recovery path in spec §3.4.5 remains as safety net. Q5 — Null default for engagement_export_dir on new engagements: dialog field empty by default with 'Optional — leave blank to disable auto-export' placeholder; null disables auto-export with log warning at activation; the dogfood migration explicitly sets a non-null value for CRMBUILDER (the engine-repo db-export/ path computed at migration time).

Operational decisions (just decided per the two-part test outcome). Q6 — engagement_last_opened_at PATCH path during the switching dance: Option B (defer PATCH until after new API subprocess is up) chosen over Option A (desktop opens meta DB directly during the kill-relaunch dance). Respects DEC-019's API-only principle for recurring operations (the dogfood migration's direct-DB-access exception is a one-shot first-launch thing; activation happens every switch). The PATCH lagging by the few hundred milliseconds between API health-check success and signal emission is imperceptible; PATCH is idempotent so health-check failure leaves the timestamp unchanged with no other consequence. Architecture doc §4 step ordering amended (step 7 moves to between steps 9 and 11; renumbered 12-step sequence documented in PRD §3 architecture summary). Q10 — Meta DB export location: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/meta/engagements.json; the meta/ subdirectory lives parallel to the dogfood's engagement-content exports at db-export/, making the scope distinction visible (install-level routing metadata vs engagement content) while keeping related artifacts together. Q7 — engagement panel refresh trigger: file-watch on db-export/meta/engagements.json per the standard v0.3+ pattern, plus subscription to active_engagement_changed Qt signals for refreshing the active-engagement marker when switching occurs without underlying engagement records changing. Q8 — activation sequence retry/timeout values: adopt architecture doc §4 defaults as written (step 4 port-release timeout 5s before SIGKILL escalation; step 9 health-check exponential backoff starting 100ms, max delay 5s, total timeout 30s; step 3 Alembic pre-flight no explicit timeout with progress indicator). Tune in slice D or post-build if observation reveals problems.

PRD authoring. Drafted PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ui-PRD-v0.5.md at 425 lines, density-matched to v0.4's 378 lines (v0.5 architecture-summary section richer because of routing infrastructure). Thirteen sections: §1 Overview (purpose, background, source decisions including DEC-039/046/068/075-077 plus DEC-078-086 plus DEC-098 through DEC-104 forthcoming, renumbering note recording the three-way collision resolution); §2 Scope (10 in-scope plus 16 out-of-scope items); §3 Architecture summary; §4 Schema summary; §5 Functional Requirements (5.1 management panel; 5.2 switching affordance; 5.3 single-gesture creation; 5.4 dogfood migration; 5.5 lazy migration; 5.6 forbid-active-delete); §6 Cross-Cutting Concerns including §6.5 User Process Guide drift acknowledgement deferring v0.2 to v0.6; §7 Acceptance Criteria including slice E closeout criteria E1-E8; §8 Implementation Plan Reference; §9 PI-001 Coordination (rewritten at closeout after styling Conversation 2 committed ui-PRD-v0.6.md mid-conversation; v0.5 ships first per DEC-095; v0.6 retrofits engagement panel as the 13th panel covered by v0.6 slice C; v0.5 surfaces ship with Qt-default styling plus TODO-for-v0.6-retrofit inline placeholder values); §10 Constraints (storage layer additive; API-only access except at activation and migration boundaries; per-engagement DB schemas unchanged; no engagement-aware MCP tools in v0.5); §11 Risks and Mitigations (10 risks each with likelihood/impact/mitigation); §12 Open Questions (5 questions deferred to build time); §13 Decisions to Be Recorded (DEC-098 through DEC-104 with renumbering note recording the collision resolution).

Implementation plan authoring. Drafted PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ui-v0.5-implementation-plan.md at 437 lines, density-matched to v0.4's 450 lines. Linear five-slice dependency chain (unlike v0.4's partial independence). New module directory layout fully specified including: access/meta_db.py, access/engagement_models.py, access/engagement.py, api/routers/engagements.py, migration/dogfood_v0_5.py, migration/lazy_migration.py, migration/meta_alembic.py, ui/active_engagement_context.py, ui/activation_worker.py, ui/widgets/engagement_top_strip.py, ui/widgets/engagement_picker.py, ui/widgets/activation_overlay.py, ui/panels/engagement_panel.py, ui/dialogs/engagement_crud.py, ui/dialogs/engagement_delete.py, ui/dialogs/new_engagement_dialog.py. Two Alembic chains: meta DB at crmbuilder-v2/migrations/meta/ (new in slice A), per-engagement chain unchanged. Test counts estimated ~165-225 new across the five slices.

Slice build prompt authoring. Drafted five prompts: CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.5-A-foundation-and-dogfood-migration.md (slice A); -B-engagement-schema-and-api.md (slice B); -C-engagement-management-panel.md (slice C); -D-engagement-switching.md (slice D); -E-closeout.md (slice E). Each prompt follows the v0.4 prompt shape (purpose, project context, pre-flight, reading order, step-by-step implementation guidance, acceptance verification, commit instructions with single-commit-Doug-pushes convention, what-NOT-to-do guardrails). Slice A includes the A1/A2 split fallback if size becomes unmanageable at execution. Slice C includes inert TODO placeholders for the 'Switch engagement' / 'Create engagement' buttons in the forbid-active-delete dialog state, with slice D rewiring documented. Slice D documents the 12-step activation sequence with the question-6 amendment (PATCH deferred to step 10). Slice prompts reference styling-design-pass.md as a reading and inheritance point but include the inline-placeholder fallback (which is the actual expected posture given v0.6's separate-release sequencing per DEC-095).

Close-out path chosen. Path 1 (Claude Code apply prompt) mirroring SES-026's precedent — the apply prompt POSTs the payload via apply_close_out.py. Per in-sandbox close-out convention from recent_updates: Claude commits AND pushes all nine deliverables (PRD + implementation plan + five slice prompts + close-out payload + apply prompt) in one commit at conversation close; Doug pulls; the apply prompt itself runs at Doug's terminal under the 'Doug pushes' convention for local-clone work.

Context compaction event. The conversation transcript exceeded the active context window during slice A prompt authoring; the conversation history was compacted to a structured summary. Post-compaction work proceeded against the summary: slice A prompt already drafted before compaction; slices B-E prompts plus close-out payload plus apply prompt drafted after compaction. Mid-way through closeout authoring, a pull from origin/main brought in the styling Conversation 2 deliverables (ui-PRD-v0.6.md, ui-v0.6-implementation-plan.md) plus the SES-027 styling apply; the payload and PRD §9 and PRD renumbering notes were updated to reflect the three-way numbering collision and v0.6's separate-release posture before commit.
session.artifacts_produced: Nine deliverables committed to origin/main in one commit by Claude per the in-sandbox close-out convention: (1) PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ui-PRD-v0.5.md (release PRD, 425 lines, draft pending approval); (2) PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ui-v0.5-implementation-plan.md (slice breakdown, 437 lines, draft pending approval); (3) PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.5-A-foundation-and-dogfood-migration.md (slice A build prompt: meta DB + ActiveEngagementContext + two-database API + lazy migration + dogfood migration + sidebar Engagements group + refresh service extension + .gitignore extensions); (4) -B-engagement-schema-and-api.md (slice B: engagement repository + eight REST endpoints + validation + JSON snapshot hook + client extensions); (5) -C-engagement-management-panel.md (slice C: management panel + CRUD dialogs + forbid-active-delete with last-engagement edge case + refresh service registration + sidebar entry); (6) -D-engagement-switching.md (slice D: top-strip widget + picker dropdown + activation overlay + 12-step ActivationWorker + single-gesture NewEngagementDialog + slice-C placeholder rewiring); (7) -E-closeout.md (slice E: version 0.5.0 bump + README release note + end-to-end integration smoke + final regression pass + manual smoke checklist); (8) PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_029.json (this payload); (9) PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-029.md (apply prompt for this payload).
session.in_flight_at_end: Slice A is the next executable Claude Code prompt. Doug runs it at his local terminal: clone or pull crmbuilder repo, open Claude Code in the repo, instruct Claude Code to execute the slice A prompt. Slice A produces the foundation infrastructure plus the dogfood migration; after slice A completes and Doug pushes, slice B is queued; and so on through slice E. Status remains 'v0.4 complete' until slice E lands and the status entity is operator-updated. The PI-001 styling workstream's v0.6 release runs in parallel per DEC-095's separate-release sequencing; v0.6's slice C retrofits the engagement panel introduced in v0.5 slice C, alongside the existing twelve panels — v0.5 ships first with Qt-default styling, v0.6 retrofits tokens onto all panels including the engagement panel. The User Process Guide v0.2 update is deferred to v0.6 per PRD §2 Out of Scope. The paper-test workstream (DEC-077) remains deferred until v0.5 ships and a CBM engagement is created via single-gesture flow.

decisions.title: v0.5 slice breakdown: five slices with foundation+migration combined
decisions.context: v0.5 Conversation 2's slicing question (Q9 in the kickoff). Workstream-plan strawman from §5 had four slices (foundation+migration combined, engagement entity panel, switching, closeout). Alternative granular framing from kickoff §5.3 had six slices (config foundation, dogfood migration standalone, schema+access+REST, panel UI, switching+mechanism, closeout). Slice count and content directly affect build-time cadence: too tight makes each prompt oversize and increases the cost of any one slice's failure mode; too granular increases prompt-and-merge overhead and creates non-functional intermediate states. The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact (build cadence and acceptance-gate granularity over the next several days of execution) AND two viable options producing meaningfully different outcomes (four vs six vs five-with-different-cuts). Decided via the consequential-decision template.
decisions.decision: Five slices: A — foundation infrastructure (meta DB, ActiveEngagementContext, two-database API wiring, lazy-migration helper, sidebar Engagements group container, refresh-service extension) PLUS dogfood migration combined in one slice; B — engagement schema (dataclass, status enum), access layer (eight standard repository methods with validation), and REST API (eight standard endpoints with envelope shape, JSON snapshot hook); C — engagement management panel UI (ListDetailPanel subclass, master/detail panes, CRUD dialogs, forbid-active-delete with last-engagement edge case, refresh-service registration); D — switching mechanism (top-strip widget, picker dropdown, 12-step ActivationWorker with question-6 amendment, activation overlay) plus single-gesture creation+activation flow (NewEngagementDialog extending slice C's EngagementCreateDialog with file creation and activation steps); E — closeout (version 0.5.0 bump, README release note, end-to-end integration smoke test, final regression pass, manual smoke verification). Slice A monitored for size at execution time; A1 (foundation only) / A2 (dogfood migration) split is the documented fallback if the slice A prompt exceeds healthy size.
decisions.rationale: Five slices balance build cadence with acceptance-gate granularity. Combining foundation with dogfood migration in slice A is justified because foundation alone is a non-functional intermediate state (the meta DB exists but no engagement records and dogfood data is still at the old path; the desktop cannot operate against this state without falling back to the old single-DB path or failing); separating them would require either a non-functional intermediate slice or a hidden fallback path in slice A's code, both of which are worse than combining. Splitting slice B (schema+API) from slice C (panel UI) is justified because each is substantial on its own — B's eight REST endpoints with full validation logic plus the JSON snapshot hook is a complete subsystem; C's master/detail panel plus three CRUD dialogs plus the forbid-active-delete edge cases is a complete UI surface — and combining them produces an oversize prompt with mixed concerns. Keeping the switching mechanism (slice D) as one slice rather than splitting picker from activation worker is justified because the picker and activation worker are tightly coupled: the picker click triggers the activation worker, the activation overlay binds to worker progress signals, and the single-gesture flow uses the activation worker as its third step; splitting would require defining a fake activation API in slice C/D and a backfill in the next slice. The A1/A2 split fallback is documented but not pre-committed; the decision is made at slice A's prompt-drafting time if size signals warrant.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option 1: four slices per the workstream-plan strawman (foundation+migration in one, engagement entity panel in another, switching, closeout). Rejected because the 'engagement entity panel' in the strawman bundled both the REST API and the panel UI, producing an oversize prompt mixing access-layer/API/validation work with UI/CRUD-dialog work. Option 2: six slices per kickoff §5.3's granular framing. Rejected because separating the dogfood migration into its own slice creates a non-functional intermediate state at slice A's completion (the foundation infrastructure exists but the dogfood is still at the old path; running v0.5 in that state requires a fallback path that adds code complexity for a state that exists for only one slice). Option 3: eight slices splitting the activation worker, the picker, and the single-gesture flow into separate slices. Rejected because the tight coupling among these components would require multiple fake-coupling-point slices.

decisions.title: Engagement UI affordance placement: top-strip switching widget plus Engagements sidebar group plus dual paths to management panel
decisions.context: v0.5 Conversation 2's two UI-placement questions (Q1 and Q2 in the kickoff), settled together because the affordances are coupled. Q1: where does the switching affordance live? Q2: where does the management affordance live? The engagement entity is structurally different from the four v0.4 methodology entity types (v2-install-level routing metadata vs domain-scope methodology content), so it doesn't fit the Methodology sidebar group introduced in v0.4. Switching is a frequent action requiring always-visible affordance; management is a less-frequent action requiring discoverable affordance. The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact (every user interaction with v0.5 touches one or both affordances) AND two viable options producing meaningfully different outcomes (top-strip vs sidebar-only vs modal). Decided via the consequential-decision template.
decisions.decision: Three coordinated affordances: (1) always-visible top-strip switching widget positioned above the sidebar entries inside the sidebar container; renders the active engagement's name with code in parentheses and a Lucide chevron-down caret; clicking opens the picker dropdown. (2) Picker dropdown anchored below the top-strip; live engagements ordered by engagement_last_opened_at descending; paused and archived rendered in color.neutral.500 and sorted to bottom within their bucket; active engagement marked with a Lucide check icon; footer item 'Manage engagements...' separated by hairline divider, opens the management panel. Empty state ('No engagement selected' top-strip; picker contains only the footer item) handled. (3) Engagements sidebar group positioned above Governance with one entry titled 'Engagements' that opens the management panel — same panel that the picker footer opens. The management panel is a ListDetailPanel subclass per the v0.4 pattern: master pane with five columns (Identifier, Code, Name, Status, Last Opened); detail pane with form fields per engagement.md §3.6.3; standard CRUD operations including the forbid-active-delete behavior per DEC-101.
decisions.rationale: The top-strip placement gives switching an always-visible affordance distinct from the sidebar's content-navigation function. Switching is potentially frequent (multiple times per work session if a consultant is working across multiple engagements); requiring a sidebar click would add friction. The Engagements sidebar group entry is the discoverability path for users who treat the sidebar as their primary navigation; without the entry, the management UI would be reachable only through the picker's footer item which is less discoverable. Dual paths to the management panel (sidebar entry + picker footer) reduce friction without creating navigational ambiguity — both paths arrive at the same panel. The sidebar group's position above Governance reflects the v2-install-level scope distinction (engagement records are routing metadata across all engagements, structurally above the per-engagement governance and methodology content). Picker ordering rules (live first by last-opened-at descending; non-live at bottom; active marked) match user expectations for an MRU-style picker. The empty-state handling is required for the fresh-install case where the picker has nothing to select from.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option B: sidebar-only with everything in the Engagements group. Rejected because switching becomes a multi-click sequence (sidebar → engagement panel → row → 'Activate' action) when it should be a single-click affordance. Option C: modal-only switching invoked from the sidebar with no top-strip. Rejected because modal-only switching breaks the user's mental model — switching is a transient action, not a configuration dialog, and forcing a modal interrupt for every switch is friction without payoff. Option D: top-strip-only without a sidebar group. Rejected because management UI discoverability suffers; new users wouldn't know where to find the panel. Option E: place the Engagements group below the existing sidebar groups instead of above. Rejected because v2-install-level scope is structurally above per-engagement scope; placement below would suggest the opposite hierarchy.

decisions.title: Single-gesture engagement creation+activation flow with graceful inline failure recovery
decisions.context: v0.5 Conversation 2's engagement-creation flow question (Q3 in the kickoff). Creating a new engagement requires three operations: POST /engagements (creates meta DB row), desktop creates per-engagement DB file with Alembic at head, desktop initiates 12-step activation. The question is whether to expose these as a single user gesture (one click triggers all three) or as separate gestures (user clicks 'Create', then later clicks 'Activate'). The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact (creation is a recurring action; the choice shapes every engagement-creation user experience) AND two viable options producing meaningfully different outcomes (single-gesture vs two-gesture). Decided via the consequential-decision template.
decisions.decision: Single-gesture creation+activation: the New Engagement dialog runs all three operations behind one user click on Submit. Three-label progress indicator advances through 'Creating engagement record...' → 'Initializing database...' → 'Switching to <name>...' Each label transitions to a Lucide check icon on success or a Lucide circle-x in color.danger.default on failure. Validation errors at each stage roll back appropriately: POST failure stays in the dialog with inline error per the v0.3 validation-error pattern; file-creation failure DELETEs the meta DB row to roll back; activation failure after both creates succeed surfaces inline with 'Try switching now' and 'Stay in <previous engagement>' affordances and the engagement record persists. The slice-C EngagementCreateDialog (which creates the meta DB row only) remains in the codebase as a parent class for NewEngagementDialog; the slice-D NewEngagementDialog is the primary path for engagement creation after slice D lands, but EngagementCreateDialog is still available for scripted use cases (e.g., creating a record without activation for testing or recovery scenarios).
decisions.rationale: Single-gesture creation matches the user's mental model: creating an engagement implies wanting to work in it; forcing a separate 'now activate it' gesture adds friction without payoff. The three-label progress indicator gives a coherent narrative for the few-second sequence; the labels advance fast enough to read as confirmation of progress rather than as a slow operation. The graceful failure-recovery affordances handle the one realistic partial-success state (engagement record and file exist but activation failed) cleanly without silent rollback; the user explicitly chooses whether to retry or to stay in the previous engagement. The slice-C dialog remaining in the codebase as a parent class enables the slice-D extension via inheritance rather than duplication, and supports edge-case use through direct invocation.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option B: two-gesture flow with explicit Create then Activate affordances. Rejected because every creator would have to choose 'now or later' twice (Create dialog and then a separate Activate action), which is friction without payoff for the dominant use case (create-then-immediately-work-in). Option C: single-gesture with all-or-nothing rollback (any failure rolls back to no-engagement-created state). Rejected because activation failure after both creates succeed is a realistic transient state (port-release timeout, MCP launch failure, etc.) where rolling back the engagement record forces re-data-entry; preserving the record with a retry-or-stay choice is better UX. Option D: single-gesture with implicit retry on activation failure. Rejected because hidden retry behavior produces opaque latency and obscures real failures from the user; explicit retry is better.

decisions.title: Forbid soft-delete on the currently-active engagement with inline redirect to switch first
decisions.context: v0.5 Conversation 2's active-engagement-soft-delete question (Q4 in the kickoff). When the user attempts to soft-delete the engagement that is currently active, two postures are possible: (a) refuse the operation and redirect to switching first, or (b) accept the operation and auto-switch to another engagement before completing the soft-delete. The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact is minor (the operation is uncommon by use-case design) AND only one option is genuinely defensible (refusing is cleaner; auto-switch introduces choosing-which-engagement-to-switch-to as a hidden decision). Decided as decide-and-announce.
decisions.decision: The engagement panel's Delete dialog detects whether the target row is the currently-active engagement (via comparison against ActiveEngagementContext.engagement_identifier). When active, the standard edge-text confirmation field is replaced with an inline message: '<engagement_name> is currently active. Switch to a different engagement first, then soft-delete this one.' The Delete button is replaced with a 'Switch engagement' button that opens the picker dropdown. Last-engagement edge case (the active engagement is the only one on the install): message changes to '<engagement_name> is the only engagement on this install. Create another engagement before soft-deleting this one.' The button changes to 'Create engagement' opening the New Engagement dialog (single-gesture variant in slice D, slice-C Create dialog before slice D lands). The next-launch drift-recovery path in engagement.md §3.4.5 remains as a safety net for cross-restart desync (e.g., user soft-deletes the active engagement via direct API call or external script, then restarts the desktop; current_engagement.json points at a soft-deleted engagement; in-memory context starts empty; picker prompts for selection).
decisions.rationale: Refusing the operation is cleaner than auto-switch: the user explicitly chooses which engagement to switch to (via the picker) rather than the system silently picking 'next most recent' or similar. Inline redirect (replacing the Delete button with 'Switch engagement') keeps the user in the same UI context, avoiding modal-stacking. The last-engagement edge case wording handles the corner case where switching isn't possible, redirecting to creation. The drift-recovery safety net handles out-of-band deletion that bypasses the dialog. The operation is uncommon by use-case design (consultants don't typically delete the engagement they're working in), so the additional friction of refuse-and-redirect is acceptable.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option B: accept the operation and auto-switch to another engagement before completing the soft-delete. Rejected because the auto-switch target is ambiguous (most-recent? alphabetical? what if there's only one other? what if it's soft-deleted?); making the user choose via the picker is cleaner. Option C: hard error with no redirect (just refuse). Rejected because the message would leave the user wondering what to do next; the redirect affordance is small additional cost for substantial UX gain.

decisions.title: Null default for engagement_export_dir on new engagement records
decisions.context: v0.5 Conversation 2's export-dir-default question (Q5 in the kickoff). The engagement_export_dir field on the engagement record is nullable per engagement.md §3.2; when set, it points at an absolute filesystem path where the engagement's JSON snapshots are auto-written. The New Engagement dialog needs to handle the default state: pre-fill with a computed path (e.g., a subdirectory of the engagement-code-named home directory)? Leave blank? The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact is minor (this is a configuration default, not a behavior change) AND only one option fits the Q8-of-Conversation-1 framing where null disables auto-export. Decided as decide-and-announce.
decisions.decision: The New Engagement dialog's export-dir field is empty by default with placeholder text 'Optional — leave blank to disable auto-export.' and tooltip 'Where this engagement's JSON snapshots will be written. Recommend a path inside the client repo so exports travel with the engagement documents.' Null is a first-class value (disables auto-export; the access-layer validates this case in slice B and the activation-time export check in slice D logs a warning if null is the value). The dogfood migration in slice A explicitly sets a non-null value for CRMBUILDER (the engine-repo db-export/ path computed at migration time); the migration's behavior is divergent from the dialog default because the dogfood engagement has a definite and computable export location while user-created engagements typically need explicit configuration of where exports should land.
decisions.rationale: Null default avoids guessing what the user wants (any pre-filled path would either need to be a sensible default for most use cases or would mislead users into accepting a value they hadn't actually chosen). The placeholder text makes the optionality explicit. The tooltip provides discoverability for the recommended pattern (path inside the client repo) without forcing it. The CRMBUILDER divergence from the dialog default is justified because the dogfood has a known export location (engine-repo db-export/) that the migration knows about; user-created engagements don't have an analogous always-correct default.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option B: pre-fill with a computed path like ~/Documents/crmbuilder/{engagement_code}/db-export/. Rejected because the path would be wrong for the dominant use case (client-repo-based) and right for an edge case (consultant-local-only). Option C: pre-fill with an absolute path computed from a configuration setting. Rejected because v0.5 has no such configuration setting and adding one for a default isn't justified.

decisions.title: Meta DB JSON exports at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/meta/engagements.json
decisions.context: v0.5 Conversation 2's meta DB export location question (Q10 in the kickoff, tightly coupled to Q7's engagement panel refresh trigger). The meta DB hosts the engagements registry table; v0.3's JSON-snapshot pattern (DEC-022, DEC-008) makes writes regenerate snapshot files under db-export/ that panels file-watch for refresh. The question is whether the meta DB participates in this pattern and where its snapshot lands. The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact is moderate (the snapshot location affects how the engagement panel refreshes and where the artifact lives in the repo) AND there are two viable options producing meaningfully different outcomes (bundled into the dogfood's db-export/ vs separate location). Decided as an operational refinement.
decisions.decision: Meta DB has its own JSON export at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/meta/engagements.json. The db-export/meta/ subdirectory lives parallel to the dogfood's engagement-content exports at db-export/ (which are sessions.json, decisions.json, planning_items.json, etc. — the CRMBUILDER engagement's content). Writes to the meta DB regenerate engagements.json via the standard access-layer hook synchronously within the same transaction. The path is fixed at the engine-repo level (not configurable per engagement, because the meta DB is install-scope routing metadata, not engagement-scope content). The engagement panel registers with the refresh service and refreshes on file change per the standard v0.3+ pattern.
decisions.rationale: Uniformity with the v0.3+ JSON-snapshot pattern: the meta DB participates in the same render-on-write discipline as per-engagement DBs. The meta/ subdirectory makes the scope distinction visible (install-level routing metadata vs engagement content) — a future reader of the db-export/ directory tree can distinguish 'this is CRMBUILDER's content' from 'this is install-level engagement registry' without reading the file contents. Bundling the meta DB export into the dogfood's db-export/engagements.json is conceptually wrong because the dogfood's per-engagement DB doesn't contain engagement records; engagement records live in the meta DB; mixing them produces a confusing artifact. Placing the meta DB export outside db-export/ entirely (e.g., at crmbuilder-v2/data/exports/) would require a separate file-watch pattern outside the v0.3+ convention.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option B: no JSON export for the meta DB; engagement panel refreshes via explicit API polling or via signals fired by access-layer writes only. Rejected because it breaks uniformity with the v0.3+ pattern and creates a special-case refresh mechanism for one panel. Option C: bundle the meta DB export into the dogfood's db-export/ as a single engagements.json at the parent level. Rejected because the dogfood's db-export/ is scoped to CRMBUILDER's content; mixing in install-level metadata produces a confusing artifact and a coupling between the dogfood and the meta DB that doesn't otherwise exist. Option D: place the meta DB export outside db-export/ entirely. Rejected because doing so departs from the established v0.3+ JSON-snapshot pattern without justification.

decisions.title: v0.5 UI PRD approval — release scope and slice plan accepted
decisions.context: v0.5 Conversation 2's PRD approval gate (Q12 implicit in the kickoff's deliverables list). The PRD at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ui-PRD-v0.5.md and the companion implementation plan at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ui-v0.5-implementation-plan.md and the five slice build prompts at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.5-{A..E}-*.md were drafted in this conversation and presented for review. The PRD opens 'Draft — pending approval'; this decision records the transition to Approved.
decisions.decision: v0.5 UI PRD v0.5 approved: 10 in-scope items (multi-engagement routing infrastructure; dogfood migration; engagement entity type with eight standard endpoints; 12-step activation sequence with question-6 amendment; Engagements sidebar group and management panel; switching affordance with top-strip and picker; single-gesture engagement creation; forbid-active-delete with last-engagement edge case; meta DB JSON exports with file-watch refresh; About-dialog version bump and README release note), 16 out-of-scope items (multi-tenant API+MCP per PI-017; engagement-level access control; v1 Client import; cross-engagement queries; optional engagement_db_path override; engagement rename; hard delete with file removal; additional engagement-level fields; User Process Guide v0.2; auto-switch on active-engagement soft-delete; right-click 'Activate' on engagement panel; full styling design pass execution; methodology entity types beyond v0.4's four; methodology entity renderers; catalog FK integration; v1 NOT_SUPPORTED reimplementation workstreams), five-slice plan (A foundation+migration combined; B engagement schema+access+REST; C panel UI; D switching+single-gesture; E closeout), 10 acceptance criteria for slice E closeout plus the 13 architecture-level and 12 entity-level acceptance criteria distributed across slices A-D. Implementation plan companion approved at the same time. Five slice build prompts approved at the same time. PRD §9 coordination section rewritten at closeout to reflect the styling Conversation 2 v0.6 PRD that landed mid-conversation: v0.5 ships first per DEC-095's separate-release sequencing; v0.6 retrofits engagement panel and all other surfaces as the 13th panel covered by v0.6 slice C. PRD transitions from Draft to Approved.
decisions.rationale: The PRD and implementation plan and five slice prompts together cover everything needed for v0.5 execution: requirements (PRD), build sequencing (implementation plan), step-by-step execution guidance (slice prompts). The release scope matches the workstream-plan and Conversation 1's architectural foundation; the slice plan optimizes for build cadence while preserving non-trivial acceptance gates; the deferred items have justified rationales (cited PIs, future versions, scope discipline). The PRD's 11 risks and mitigations and 5 open questions are appropriate for a build-time-tunable release. The §9 rewrite captures the actual coordination posture with v0.6 (which crystallized mid-conversation when the styling Conversation 2 deliverables landed on origin/main) rather than the pre-clarification posture that the original draft assumed.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Alternative scope shapes were not explicitly evaluated as alternatives — the scope was iteratively refined during the conversation through the ten settled questions and the consequential-decision presentations. The PRD shape (13 sections vs the kickoff's suggested 8-section shape) was chosen to match v0.4's shape per the kickoff's 'Shape follows ui-PRD-v0.4.md' direction.
